introduction.title: Vietnamese Questions
content: Vietnamese has three main types of questions: questions with question words (wh-questions), yes-no questions, and rhetorical questions.

Unlike English, Vietnamese question words stay in their logical position in the sentence—there is no 'wh-movement' to the front. Yes-no questions are formed by adding particles at the end of statements.

questionTypes.title: Questions with Question Words
questionTypes.description: Vietnamese question words (what, who, where, when, why, how) stay in their logical position in the sentence. This is called 'wh-in-situ'—the question word remains where its answer would appear.

notes: With classifier: 'Cái gì' is more specific — 'Cái gì đây?' (What is this?)
After nouns to narrow type: 'sách gì' (what book), 'món gì' (what dish)
In negatives means 'anything/nothing': 'Không có gì' (There's nothing), 'Không sao gì' (It's nothing/It's fine)
'Gì cũng' = anything/whatever: 'Ăn gì cũng được' (I'll eat anything)
'Của ai' for possession: 'Sách của ai?' (Whose book?)
In negatives/questions means 'anyone': 'Có ai không?' (Is anyone there?)
'Ai cũng' = everyone: 'Ai cũng biết' (Everyone knows)
Works as both subject and object without changing form
'Ở đâu' for static location: 'Nhà ở đâu?' (Where is the house?)
'Đi đâu' for direction/destination: 'Bạn đi đâu?' (Where are you going?)
'Đâu cũng' = everywhere: 'Đâu cũng có' (It's everywhere)
Also used as rhetorical negation: 'Đâu có!' (Not at all! / No way!)
Most common in daily speech, often implies anticipation: 'Bao giờ xong?' (When will it be done?)
'Khi nào' is more formal/neutral — preferred in writing and official contexts
With negation, 'bao giờ' means 'never': 'Tôi không bao giờ hút thuốc' (I never smoke) — cannot use 'khi nào' here
Position doesn't change meaning in questions, but tone can imply impatience: 'Bao giờ cưới?' (When are you getting married?)
Most common in daily conversation — can start the sentence unlike most question words
'Tại sao' is formal (writing, news, official): 'Tại sao dự án thất bại?' (Why did the project fail?)
'Vì sao' is neutral, slightly literary — common in essays and explanations
All three are interchangeable in meaning, only tone/register differs
'Như thế nào' is the full/formal form: 'Tình hình như thế nào?' (How is the situation?)
For asking about methods, use 'Bằng cách nào': 'Làm bằng cách nào?' (How do you do it?)
Often shortened to 'sao' in casual speech: 'Bạn thấy sao?' (How do you feel about it?)
'Thế nào cũng' = no matter how: 'Thế nào cũng được' (Any way is fine)
Combines with classifiers: 'cuốn nào' (which book), 'con nào' (which animal), 'người nào' (which person)
'Cái nào' is the default when you don't know the classifier
Also used as 'let's/come on': 'Đi nào!' (Let's go!), 'Nào, bắt đầu!' (Come on, let's start!)
'Nào cũng' = whichever: 'Cái nào cũng được' (Any one is fine)
For large or unknown quantities — no upper limit assumed
'Mấy' for small expected quantities (roughly under 10): 'Mấy giờ?' (What time?), 'Mấy tuổi?' (How old? — for children)
'Bao lâu' for duration: 'Bạn ở bao lâu?' (How long are you staying?)
'Bao xa' for distance: 'Từ đây đến đó bao xa?' (How far is it from here?)

patterns.title: Question Word Replaces the Answer
patterns.description: Unlike English, Vietnamese doesn't move question words to the front. The question word sits exactly where the answer would go. If it's the subject, it's at the front. If it's the object or location, it stays there.
patterns.structure: Statement with [answer] → Replace [answer] with question word

examples.notes: 'gì' replaces the object — answer: 'Bạn đọc sách trinh thám' (You read a mystery book)
'Cái gì' replaces the subject — answer: 'Âm nhạc làm bạn vui' (Music makes you happy)
'Ai' replaces the subject — answer: 'Nam nói với em' (Nam talked to you)
'của ai' replaces the possessor — answer: 'Đây là xe của Nam' (This is Nam's vehicle)

examples.vietnamese: Anh ấy mua cái này ở đâu?
examples.notes: 'đâu' replaces the location — answer: 'Anh ấy mua cái này ở chợ' (He bought this at the market)
'Sao' is the exception — it starts the sentence like in English

examples.vietnamese: Tại sao bạn học tiếng Việt?
examples.notes: Formal variant of 'sao' — same meaning, more polished register

examples.vietnamese: Vì sao anh ấy nghỉ việc?
examples.english: Why did he quit his job?
examples.notes: Neutral/literary variant of 'sao' — between casual 'sao' and formal 'tại sao'
'Bao giờ' replaces the time — can also go at the end: 'Chúng ta đi bao giờ?'

examples.english: How do you feel about it?
examples.notes: 'thế nào' replaces the manner — answer: 'Bạn thấy tốt' (You feel good)
'nào' follows the classifier — answer: 'Bạn chọn cuốn này' (You choose this one)

examples.vietnamese: Lớp có bao nhiêu học sinh?
examples.english: How many students are in the class?
examples.notes: 'bao nhiêu' replaces the quantity — answer: 'Lớp có ba mươi học sinh' (The class has thirty students)

examples.vietnamese: Khi nào dự án hoàn thành?
examples.english: When will the project be completed?
examples.notes: Formal variant of 'bao giờ' — preferred in work/official contexts
Full/formal form of 'thế nào' — common in news and official speech

questionTypes.title: Yes-No Questions
questionTypes.description: Yes-no questions are formed by adding particles at the end of a statement. Different particles convey different levels of certainty or expectation.

notes: The most basic yes-no particle — completely neutral, no assumption about the answer
Answer with the verb to say yes: 'Thích' (Yes/Like), or 'Không' to say no
Can also add 'có' before the verb for emphasis: 'Bạn có thích không?' (same meaning, slightly more formal)
Tag question — you think the answer is yes and want confirmation
Like English 'right?' or 'isn't it?'
'Phải' literally means 'must/correct', so 'phải không' = 'correct or not?'
Very similar to 'phải không' — slightly more direct
'Đúng' means 'correct/right', so 'đúng không' = 'correct or not?'
Often used when checking facts: 'Hôm nay thứ hai đúng không?' (Today is Monday, right?)
Specifically for permission or feasibility — not a general yes-no question
'Được' means 'can/okay', so 'được không' = 'okay or not?'
Very common and polite: 'Cho tôi xem được không?' (Can I take a look?)
You already believe the answer is yes — just confirming
Stronger expectation than 'không' — implies 'obviously, right?'
Very common in casual speech: 'Hiểu chứ?' (You understand, right?)
Stronger than 'phải không' — you expect a definite yes
Can carry slight pressure: 'Em sẽ cố gắng phải chứ?' (You'll try hard, right?)
The 'chứ' adds conviction that 'không' doesn't have
Expecting strong agreement — you're quite sure you're correct
More assertive than 'đúng không'
Often used when making a point: 'Chúng ta phải thay đổi đúng chứ?' (We need to change, right?)
Assumes permission — less of a request than 'được không'
'Được không' asks politely, 'được chứ' expects a yes
Compare: 'Tôi ngồi đây được không?' (May I?) vs 'Tôi ngồi đây được chứ?' (I can sit here, right?)
Soft, friendly — inviting the listener to share your feeling
Very common between friends: 'Vui nhỉ?' (Fun, huh?), 'Lâu rồi nhỉ?' (It's been a while, huh?)
Not really asking a question — more like sharing an observation
Expresses surprise or mild disbelief — 'wait, really?'
Also used to ask someone to repeat what they said.
'hả' is more common in the South, 'hở' in the North.

examples.notes: Neutral question, no assumption about answer

examples.vietnamese: Bạn là sinh viên phải không?
examples.notes: Seeking confirmation — speaker thinks the answer is yes

examples.vietnamese: Hôm nay thứ hai đúng không?
examples.notes: Checking a fact — slightly more direct than 'phải không'
Asking for permission — polite request
Speaker already thinks it's delicious — just sharing the feeling
Strong expectation — speaker wants a definite yes

examples.vietnamese: Chúng ta phải thay đổi đúng chứ?
examples.notes: Assertive — speaker is making a point, not really asking

examples.vietnamese: Tôi mượn cái này được chứ?
examples.notes: Assumes permission — compare with the more polite 'được không'

examples.english: The weather is nice, isn't it?
examples.notes: Friendly, inviting agreement — sharing an observation
Surprised — didn't expect this information

questionTypes.title: Rhetorical Questions
questionTypes.description: Rhetorical questions express opinions or feelings without expecting an answer. These phrases are always placed at the beginning of the sentence to signal that no real answer is expected.

notes: Expects agreement — the speaker believes the answer is obviously yes
'Chẳng' is a literary/emphatic form of 'không' (not)
Often paired with 'sao' at the end: 'Chẳng phải ... sao?' for stronger rhetoric
More formal/written than casual speech
Expresses disbelief or shock — the speaker finds the situation hard to accept
Literally 'Could there be a reason that...'
Stronger emotional charge than 'phải chăng' — implies the answer should be no
Common in both speech and writing when expressing surprise
Contemplative, philosophical tone — the speaker is musing, not demanding
'Chăng' is a literary question particle (rarely used outside rhetorical contexts)
More literary/poetic than everyday speech
Often used in essays, literature, and reflective writing
Expresses uncertainty or wonder — softer and more genuine than other forms
Add 'rằng' for more formality: 'Liệu rằng ngày mai sẽ tốt đẹp hơn?' (I wonder if tomorrow will be better?)
Can also be used in genuine (non-rhetorical) wondering: 'Liệu có kịp không?' (I wonder if we'll make it in time?)
Most versatile of the rhetorical phrases — works in both speech and writing

examples.vietnamese: Chẳng phải đã đến lúc chúng ta phải hành động?
examples.english: Isn't it time we take action?
examples.literalEnglish: Isn't it has arrived time we must act?
examples.notes: Rhetorical question emphasizing urgency

examples.vietnamese: Có lẽ nào bạn không biết điều này?
examples.english: How could you not know this?
examples.literalEnglish: Could it be you not know this?
examples.notes: Expressing surprise or disbelief

examples.vietnamese: Phải chăng đây là ý nghĩa thật sự của cuộc sống?
examples.english: Could this be the true meaning of life?
examples.literalEnglish: Could it be this is meaning true of life?
examples.notes: Philosophical, contemplative tone

examples.english: I wonder if we'll make it in time?
examples.notes: Casual wondering — 'liệu' without 'rằng'

examples.vietnamese: Liệu rằng ngày mai sẽ tốt đẹp hơn?
examples.english: I wonder if tomorrow will be better?
examples.literalEnglish: Wonder if tomorrow will beautiful more?
examples.notes: More formal — 'liệu rằng' adds literary weight
